content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of NNN elements. In one operation you can swap any two elements if they are **not** adjacent.

That is, you can pick two indices iii and jjj (1≤i<j≤N1 \leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N) such that j−i>1j-i\gt 1j−i>1, and swap AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​.

Using this operation finitely many (possibly, zero) times, is it possible to turn AAA into a sorted array?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, denoting the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `YES`, if you can sort the array AAA in finitely (possibly zero) operations, and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YeS`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `YES` will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the array can be sorted using any number of swaps of non‑adjacent elements, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: array is already sorted, so YES.\nTest case 2: N=2 and the two elements are adjacent; no swap is allowed and the array is unsorted, so NO.\nTest case 3: N=3, sorted version is [2,6,6]; the middle element (6) stays in place, therefore sorting is possible, so YES.
For N=1 the array is trivially sorted.\nFor N=4 the graph of allowed swaps is connected, thus any permutation (including the sorted one) can be achieved.
Sorted array would be [1,2,3]; the middle element changes from 3 to 2, which is impossible because position 2 cannot move when N=3.

solution.approach:
The allowed operation swaps any two positions whose distance is greater than 1. Consider a graph whose vertices are the positions 1…N and whose edges connect every pair of non‑adjacent positions. A transposition (i j) is allowed exactly when there is an edge (i, j). It is a known fact that the set of transpositions corresponding to the edges of a *connected* graph generates the whole symmetric group S_N. Therefore:
- N = 1: the array is already sorted.
- N = 2: no swap is allowed, so the array must already be sorted.
- N = 3: the graph has only one edge (1,3); position 2 is isolated, thus the element at index 2 can never move. The array is sortable iff the middle element is already the same as in the sorted order.
- N >= 4: the graph of allowed swaps is connected, hence any permutation (in particular the sorted order) can be reached.
The algorithm follows directly from these observations.
For each test case:
1. If N <= 2, check whether the array is already non‑decreasing.
2. Else if N == 3, sort a copy of the array and compare the middle element with the original middle element.
3. Else (N >= 4) answer YES.
The sorting step costs O(N log N), which is trivial for N ≤ 1000.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case because of sorting when N == 3; all other cases are O(N) for the initial scan. With the given limits this easily fits within time limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the temporary sorted copy (only needed when N == 3).
